Re:Learning JS From Zero — Day 7: Functions, Your Reusable Code Wrappers
Day 7 of getting reincarnated into JS land. This time we're talking about functions. Functions are one of those things you really have to understand to write code, so let's get into it.
Every programming language has functions (or methods? whatever you want to call them). So what are they actually for?

Say you've got some action that prints out 1 to 100, and you need to use it in a bunch of places — the homepage, a product list, a checkout screen, and so on. If you don't use a function, then for every page you'd be copy-pasting that same chunk of code over and over. That's a maintenance nightmare. Imagine your boss walks over and says "actually, make it 1 to 200." Now if you've got 20 pages, you're editing 20 places — and good luck not missing one.

So you can think of a function as a way to wrap up some functionality you've already written. You just call it and run it, and it does the thing you prepared in advance. Usually the stuff inside a function is the more repetitive code, and you just import it into whatever page needs it. That's kind of hard to picture in the abstract, so here's a real example — this is an AJAX helper I wrote a while back for practice. I needed it in five or six places, so I wrapped it up into a function:
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
So all I have to do is call it, run it, and pass in the right arguments — and I can reuse that functionality as many times as I want. This is basically what people mean when they talk about an API.
The basic way to define a function looks like this ↓
This style of defining a function is called a
— meaning, a function that has a name. You can call and run it using that name. There's another way to define functions too, called an
, but I'm not going to cover that here — that comes in a later chapter.
Usually you get to decide whether your function takes any parameters, so leaving it empty is totally fine. But there's one kind of parameter you don't have to pass a value for:
. So where does this event thing show up? Let me tweak the event example from an earlier chapter a bit.

You can see the function inside the event listener takes an
. The contents are basically the history of what you just did — think of it like a time-channel of your action. So
represents the state of the event: where it was triggered, the timeline, the coordinates, all that.
So why bring this up only now? Because it makes more sense to explain it alongside
Back in the events chapter I just wanted to focus on events
. Generally speaking, when the first parameter of your event listener callback doesn't get a value passed in, it defaults to the event (Event).
Let's go back to that basic function-definition image ↓
Once we've declared a function, calling and running it looks like this ↓
So with that idea, we can take a bunch of repetitive code and wrap it into a single function. For example, wrapping up some greeting code and passing in a name:
// if you pass in a string, wrap it in single or double quotes
Now we can reuse this functionality over and over. What if we
use a function to say hi? What would happen?

You can see we end up typing way more, and reusability drops a lot. If you had 100 pages using this, then changing the greeting style means editing 100 places. It's hard to see the point with such a tiny example, but in real-world development you'll often wrap repetitive code snippets into functions. First, it saves you from writing the same code again and again. Second, when you need to change something, you only have to change it in one place.
In the next post of Re:Learning JS From Zero, we'll cover anonymous functions and IIFEs (immediately invoked function expressions).

Day 7 of my JS isekai. This time we're getting into functions — one of those things you absolutely have to understand to write code. We'll look at why functions exist, how to define a named function, what that mysterious event parameter is, and how wrapping repetitive code saves you from editing the same thing 100 times.
